steps.title: De Fiets
steps.desc: Welcome to Unit 6: getting around. And in the Netherlands, getting around starts with one thing — de fiets. There are more bicycles than people in this country. This lesson teaches cycling vocabulary, basic prepositions of direction, and why the bike IS Dutch identity.
goals: Why the Dutch and bikes are inseparable

steps.note:
De-word. THE Dutch object.
23 million bikes for 17 million people.
Plural: fietsen. Verb: fietsen (to cycle).

steps.example: Ik fiets elke dag naar het werk.
steps.exampleEn: I cycle to work every day.
steps.note:
Regular verb. Stam = fiets.
Ik fiets, jij fietst, hij fietst.
70% of Dutch commutes involve a bicycle.

Irregular verb! Your most important movement verb.
ik ga, jij gaat, hij gaat, wij gaan.
Same root as English 'go' and German 'gehen'.

steps.title: Gaan: to go
steps.label: Full Conjugation
steps.note:
ik ga (short). jij/hij gaat (long 'aa' + t). wij gaan.
In questions: 'Ga jij...?' — the -t drops as always.

Direction = naar. 'I go TO the station.'
'Naar' = towards a destination.
Don't confuse with 'aan' (at/attached to).

Two meanings: 'from' (origin) and 'of' (possession).
van het station = from the station.
de fiets van mijn broer = my brother's bike.

You learned 'met' = with (koffie met melk).
For transport: 'met de fiets' = by bike.
'Met de trein' = by train. 'Met de auto' = by car.

steps.example: Hij gaat met de auto naar het werk.
steps.exampleEn: He goes to work by car.
steps.note:
De-word. Same as German.
Less popular than the fiets in Dutch cities.
Parking in Amsterdam is €7.50/hour.

steps.also: ⚠️ False friend: Dutch 'lopen' = to WALK. German 'laufen' = to RUN.
steps.note:
Regular verb. Stam = loop.
⚠️ German speakers: 'lopen' = walk, NOT run!
Dutch 'rennen' = to run.

De-word. Plural: straten.
Identical root across Germanic languages.

steps.title: 🇳🇱 The Netherlands = Bikes
steps.text:
Some facts about Dutch cycling culture:

• 23 million bikes for 17 million people
• Dedicated bike lanes (fietspaden) everywhere
• Children learn to cycle at age 3-4
• Rain doesn't stop cyclists (nothing does)
• Bike parking garages hold 10,000+ bikes
• Amsterdam Central Station has the largest bike parking in the world

How to say it:
Ik ga met de fiets. = I go by bike.
Ik fiets naar het werk. = I cycle to work.
Ik fiets elke dag. = I cycle every day.

Not cycling in the Netherlands is like not walking.
The fiets is not transport. It's identity.

deepDive.title: Types of Dutch bikes
deepDive.text:
de stadsfiets = city bike (upright, practical, heavy)
de omafiets = 'grandma bike' (classic Dutch style)
de bakfiets = cargo bike (with a box in front for kids/groceries)
de e-bike = electric bike (huge among older Dutch people)
de racefiets = road bike (for sport)

The omafiets is the icon: black, heavy, one gear, indestructible.
Every Dutch person has had one.

The bakfiets is the Dutch SUV: parents use it to transport
2-3 children through Amsterdam traffic. Fearlessly.

steps.q: There are more ___ than people in the Netherlands.

steps.s: Ik ga {1} de fiets {2} het werk.
Hij ___ met de auto naar Amsterdam.

steps.title: Links, Rechts, Rechtdoor
steps.desc: How to give and understand directions. The three magic words: links (left), rechts (right), rechtdoor (straight ahead). Plus the verbs and phrases you need to navigate any Dutch city on foot or by bike.

steps.note:
Same word as German!
Note: Dutch drives on the RIGHT side of the road.
'Linksaf' = turning left.

steps.exampleEn: The station is on the right.
steps.note:
Same as German. The 'ch' = throat sound.
'Rechtsaf' = turning right.

recht (straight) + door (through) = straight ahead.
The most useful direction word.
'Ga rechtdoor tot het einde.' = Go straight to the end.

steps.example: Op de hoek is een winkel.
steps.exampleEn: On the corner there is a shop.
steps.note:
De-word. 'Om de hoek' = around the corner.
Related to English 'hook' (angle/bend).

De-word. The Netherlands has ~20,000 bridges.
Amsterdam alone has 1,700.
'Over de brug' = over/across the bridge.

Same as English 'over'.
'Over de straat' = across the street.
'Over de brug' = over the bridge.

steps.exampleEn: Go straight ahead until the bridge.
steps.note:
'Tot' = until (endpoint).
'Tot ziens!' = Until seeing! (goodbye)
You already know this from 'tot ziens'!

steps.exampleEn: Take the first street on the left.
steps.note:
Ordinal number: eerste = first.
Tweede = second, derde = third.
You'll learn more ordinals later.

steps.exampleEn: Take the second street on the right.
steps.note:
Irregular verb. Stam = neem.
ik neem, jij neemt, hij neemt.
For directions: 'Neem de eerste/tweede straat...'

steps.title: 🧭 Giving Directions in Dutch
steps.text:
The essential direction phrases:

Ga links. = Go left.
Ga rechts. = Go right.
Ga rechtdoor. = Go straight.
Ga over de brug. = Go over the bridge.

Neem de eerste straat links. = Take the first street left.
Neem de tweede straat rechts. = Take the second street right.

Ga rechtdoor tot de hoek. = Go straight to the corner.
Het is om de hoek. = It's around the corner.
Het is aan de linkerkant. = It's on the left side.
Het is aan de rechterkant. = It's on the right side.

These 10 phrases will get you anywhere.

steps.s: Ga {1} de brug. Het is om de {2}.
steps.hint: Go OVER the bridge. Around the CORNER.

steps.q: Amsterdam has approximately how many bridges?
'Neem de tweede straat rechts' — 'neem' is the stam of:

steps.title: Met de Trein
steps.desc: Dutch trains (NS) connect every city. They're efficient, frequent — and the Dutch love to complain about them. This lesson teaches train vocabulary and separable verbs of movement: instappen (get in), uitstappen (get out), overstappen (transfer).
goals: Buy a ticket and understand announcements

steps.example: De trein naar Amsterdam vertrekt om drie uur.
steps.exampleEn: The train to Amsterdam departs at three o'clock.
steps.note:
De-word. Plural: treinen.
'Met de trein' = by train.
NS (Nederlandse Spoorwegen) runs the national trains.

steps.example: Het station is om de hoek.
steps.exampleEn: The station is around the corner.
steps.note:
Het-word. Same as English/French.
Amsterdam Centraal is the main hub.
Every Dutch city has at least one station.

steps.example: De trein vertrekt van perron vijf.
steps.exampleEn: The train departs from platform five.
steps.note:
Het-word. From French.
'Perron 3' = Platform 3.
Announced before every departure.

Het-word (diminutive of 'kaart').
'Een kaartje' = a ticket.
The OV-chipkaart is the Dutch transit card.

steps.example: We stappen in de trein in.
steps.note:
Separable: in + stappen.
Stappen = to step. In = in/into.
'Stap in!' = Get in! Board!

Separable: uit + stappen.
Opposite of instappen.
'Stap uit!' = Get out! Exit!

steps.exampleEn: You have to transfer in Amersfoort.
steps.note:
Separable: over + stappen.
'Overstappen' is the bane of Dutch commuters.
With modal: 'Je moet overstappen' (stays whole).

steps.example: De trein vertrekt om half vier.
steps.exampleEn: The train departs at half past three.
steps.note:
NOT separable! 'ver-' is an inseparable prefix.
'De trein vertrekt.' (never 'trekt ... ver')
Compare: in/uit/over = separable. ver- = inseparable.

steps.example: Wij komen om vijf uur aan.
steps.note:
Separable: aan + komen.
'Hoe laat komt de trein aan?'
= What time does the train arrive?

steps.title: 🚆 NS: Dutch Trains
steps.text:
NS (Nederlandse Spoorwegen) runs Dutch trains.

The good: frequent (every 10-15 min between major cities),
clean, reliable (usually).

The bad: the Dutch LOVE to complain about NS.
'De trein heeft vijf minuten vertraging.'
= The train has 5 minutes delay.
This is genuinely considered a scandal.

Useful phrases:
Een kaartje naar Amsterdam. = A ticket to Amsterdam.
Van welk perron? = From which platform?
Moet ik overstappen? = Do I need to transfer?
Hoe laat komt de trein aan? = What time does the train arrive?

The OV-chipkaart: a reloadable card for all public transport
(trains, buses, trams, metro). Tap in, tap out.

deepDive.title: In/uit/over: the separable trio
deepDive.text:
These three verbs share 'stappen' (to step):

instappen = step IN (board)
uitstappen = step OUT (exit)
overstappen = step OVER (transfer)

All three are separable:
Ik stap in de trein in.
Ik stap in Utrecht uit.
Ik stap in Amersfoort over.

With modals, they stay whole:
Ik moet in Amersfoort overstappen.
Je kan in Den Haag uitstappen.

Compare: vertrekken (ver- prefix) is NOT separable.
'De trein vertrekt.' (never splits)

Rule of thumb:
Stressed prefix (ín-stappen) = separable.
Unstressed prefix (ver-trékken) = inseparable.

steps.s: Wij stappen in Utrecht {1}. De trein {2} om vier uur.
steps.hint: Exit at Utrecht. The train departs.
Transfer at Amersfoort (with modal = stays whole)

steps.title: Waar is het Station?
steps.desc: Asking for and understanding directions. You know links/rechts/rechtdoor. Now learn to ask 'where is...?' and understand the answers. Plus: the word 'er' as a location placeholder — one of the trickiest small words in Dutch.

steps.note:
You learned this in Unit 1.
'Waar?' = Where?
'Waar is...?' = Where is...? The most useful question for navigation.

Identical to German.
Same root as English 'here'.
'Hier in de buurt' = around here / nearby.

Points to a specific place.
'Daar rechts' = there on the right.
'Daar links' = there on the left.

steps.example: Er is een station in de buurt.
steps.note:
The most versatile small word in Dutch.
You already know: 'Er is een kat.' = There is a cat.
Here: 'er' as a vague location reference.
'Hoe kom ik er?' = How do I get there?

dicht (close) + bij (at) = nearby.
Opposite: ver (far) or veraf (far away).
'Is het dichtbij?' = Is it nearby?

Opposite of dichtbij.
'Is het ver?' = Is it far?
'Niet ver' = not far. Very useful reassurance.

steps.example: Hier in de buurt is een supermarkt.
steps.note:
De-word. 'In de buurt' = in the area / nearby.
'Hier in de buurt' = around here.
Also: 'de buurman' (neighbor) comes from 'buurt'.

steps.title: 🗺️ Asking for Directions
steps.text:
Excuse me + question:
Sorry, waar is het station? = Sorry, where is the station?
Pardon, is er een supermarkt in de buurt?
= Pardon, is there a supermarket nearby?

Useful follow-up questions:
Is het ver? = Is it far?
Is het dichtbij? = Is it nearby?
Hoe kom ik er? = How do I get there?
Kun je dat herhalen? = Can you repeat that?

Useful answers to understand:
Het is daar. = It's there.
Het is om de hoek. = It's around the corner.
Het is vijf minuten lopen. = It's a 5-minute walk.
Ga rechtdoor en dan links. = Go straight and then left.

steps.note:
Connects steps in directions.
'Eerst rechtdoor, dan links, dan rechts.'
= First straight, then left, then right.

De-word. Plural: minuten.
'Vijf minuten lopen' = five minutes walking.
'Tien minuten fietsen' = ten minutes cycling.

steps.s: Ga rechtdoor en {1} links. Het is vijf minuten {2}.
Is ___ een supermarkt in de buurt?

steps.q: 'Het is tien minuten fietsen.' — fietsen here means:

steps.title: Op, In, Aan, Bij
steps.desc: Prepositions are the glue of Dutch sentences. You've used naar, van, met, over, tot. Now the big four location prepositions: op (on), in (in), aan (at/on), bij (at/near). Each has its own logic — and its own surprises.

steps.also: also: 'op maandag' (on Monday), 'op het werk' (at work)
steps.example: Het boek ligt op de tafel.
steps.exampleEn: The book lies on the table.
steps.note:
Surface contact: 'op de tafel' (on the table).
Specific places: 'op het werk' (at work).
Times: 'op maandag' (on Monday — you know this!).

steps.example: De melk is in de koelkast.
steps.exampleEn: The milk is in the fridge.
steps.note:
Containment: inside something.
'In de keuken' = in the kitchen.
'In Amsterdam' = in Amsterdam.
'In de winter' = in winter (you know this!).

steps.example: Het schilderij hangt aan de muur.
steps.exampleEn: The painting hangs on the wall.
steps.note:
Attached/along: 'aan de muur' (on the wall — attached).
Along: 'aan de gracht' (on/along the canal).
'Aan de linkerkant' = on the left side.

Proximity or someone's place:
'Bij de dokter' = at the doctor.
'Bij oma' = at grandma's (place).
'Bij de ingang' = near the entrance.

steps.title: 📐 Op vs In vs Aan vs Bij
steps.text:
OP = on a surface / at a specific place
op de tafel = on the table
op het station = at the station
op het werk = at work
op school = at school

IN = inside / within
in de koelkast = in the fridge
in de stad = in the city
in Amsterdam = in Amsterdam
in de auto = in the car

AAN = attached to / along
aan de muur = on the wall (hanging)
aan de gracht = along the canal
aan de linkerkant = on the left side
aan tafel = at the table (dining)

BIJ = near / at someone's place
bij het station = near the station
bij de ingang = near the entrance
bij oma = at grandma's place
bij de huisarts = at the doctor

⚠️ Many are fixed expressions — learn the combinations,
not just the individual prepositions.

deepDive.title: The tricky ones: op vs in vs aan
deepDive.text:
Some choices seem arbitrary but have logic:

'op het station' vs 'in de trein'
You're ON the platform but IN the vehicle.

'aan de muur' vs 'op de tafel'
The painting is attached TO the wall (aan).
The book rests ON the table surface (op).

'op school' vs 'in de school'
'Op school' = at school (the institution).
'In de school' = inside the school building.

These follow the same logic as German auf/in/an
and somewhat like English on/in/at.

When in doubt: learn the expression as a chunk,
not as individual words.

steps.example: Het eten staat op de tafel.
steps.exampleEn: The food is on the table.
steps.note:
De-word. Plural: tafels.
Same word as German 'Tafel'.

steps.example: Het schilderij hangt aan de muur.
steps.exampleEn: The painting hangs on the wall.
steps.note:
De-word. Related to English 'mural'.
'Aan de muur' = on the wall (attached).
Not 'op de muur' (that implies sitting on top of it).

De-word. Nearly identical to English 'door'.
Same root as 'through' (Dutch: door) — a door is
what you go THROUGH. English split the words;
Dutch uses 'door' for both the noun and 'through'.
'Bij de deur' = near/at the door.

steps.q: 'Het boek ligt OP de tafel.' — 'op' here means:

steps.s: De melk is {1} de koelkast. Het boek ligt {2} de tafel.
steps.hint: Milk is INSIDE. Book is ON surface.
At work = op het werk (fixed expression)

steps.q: 'Op school' vs 'in de school' — the difference is:
opts: op = the institution, in = the building
op is formal, in is casual
steps.ans: op = the institution, in = the building
steps.hint: At school (concept) vs inside the school (place)
Sitting at the table (dining) = aan tafel

steps.q: Which preposition means 'near' or 'at someone's place'?

steps.title: Even naar de Winkel
steps.desc: 'Even' is the most Dutch word you haven't learned yet. It means 'just (quickly)' and the Dutch put it in EVERYTHING. 'Ik ga even naar de winkel.' This lesson teaches 'even', quick errand vocabulary, and more everyday movement phrases.

steps.example: Ik ga even naar de winkel.
steps.exampleEn: I'm just going to the shop quickly.
steps.note:
THE most Dutch filler word.
'Even' softens everything. It says: this is quick, casual, no big deal.
'Wacht even' = wait a moment.
'Mag ik even?' = May I just...?

steps.example: Ik ga even naar de supermarkt.
steps.exampleEn: I'm just popping to the supermarket.
steps.note:
De-word. Albert Heijn (AH) is the biggest chain.
'De AH' = the Albert Heijn. Every Dutch person knows it.

steps.example: De apotheek is op de hoek.
steps.exampleEn: The pharmacy is on the corner.
steps.note:
De-word. In NL, you need the huisarts (GP) first,
then a recept (prescription), then the apotheek.
No buying prescription medicine without it.

steps.example: Ik koop brood bij de bakker.
steps.exampleEn: I buy bread at the bakery.
steps.note:
De-word. 'Bij de bakker' = at the baker's.
Dutch bakeries: vers brood (fresh bread),
taarten, and kroketten (croquettes).

steps.en: to get / to fetch / to pick up
steps.exampleEn: I'm just going to get bread.
steps.note:
Regular verb. Stam = haal.
'Brood halen' = to get bread.
'Koffie halen' = to get coffee.
Also: 'afhalen' = to pick up (separable).

steps.example: Ik breng je naar het station.
steps.exampleEn: I'll bring you to the station.
steps.note:
Regular verb. Stam = breng.
Identical to English 'bring'.
'Ik breng de kinderen naar school.' = I take the kids to school.

steps.title: 🇳🇱 'Even': The Most Dutch Word
steps.text:
'Even' makes everything casual and friendly:

Ik ga even naar de winkel. = I'm just popping to the shop.
Wacht even. = Wait a moment.
Mag ik even? = May I just...?
Kun je even helpen? = Can you just help (for a sec)?
Ik ga even koffie halen. = I'm just getting coffee.
Even kijken... = Let me just look...

'Even' says: this is quick, this is casual, no big deal.
It's the verbal equivalent of a shrug.

The Dutch use 'even' 20+ times a day.
Once you start noticing it, you can't stop.
You'll start using it yourself within a week.

steps.note:
'Terug' = back (direction).
'Ik ga terug' = I go back.
'Ik ben zo terug' = I'll be right back.
'Zo' = so/soon — 'in a moment'.

steps.example: Gaan we samen naar de winkel?
steps.exampleEn: Shall we go to the shop together?
steps.note:
'Samen' = together.
'Samen eten' = eat together.
'Samen fietsen' = cycle together.
Related to English 'same'.

steps.s: Ik ga even brood {1}. Ik ben zo {2}.

steps.hint: At the baker's = bij de bakker

steps.title: Verdwaald!
steps.desc: You're lost in a Dutch city. This lesson puts all of Unit 6 together in a realistic scenario: asking strangers for help, understanding their directions, and finding your way. One extended dialogue with exercises between each section.

steps.note:
Past participle of 'verdwalen' (to get lost).
'Ik ben verdwaald' = I am lost.
A very useful sentence for tourists.

Regular verb. Stam = help.
'Kunt u mij helpen?' = formal request for help.
'Kun je me helpen?' = informal.

Regular verb. Stam = zoek.
'Ik zoek...' = I'm looking for...
No preposition needed! (Unlike English 'look FOR')

steps.example: Weet u waar het station is?
steps.exampleEn: Do you know where the station is?
steps.note:
Irregular: ik weet, jij weet, hij weet, wij weten.
All singular forms = weet.
'Ik weet het niet.' = I don't know.

steps.example: Misschien is het om de hoek.
steps.note:
Long word but very useful.
'Misschien' = perhaps.
Often starts a suggestion.

steps.title: 🗺️ Lost in the Netherlands: A Dialogue
steps.text:
YOU: Sorry, kunt u mij helpen?
= Sorry, can you help me?

STRANGER: Ja, natuurlijk! Wat zoek je?
= Yes, of course! What are you looking for?

YOU: Ik zoek het Rijksmuseum. Ik ben verdwaald.
= I'm looking for the Rijksmuseum. I'm lost.

STRANGER: Ah, dat is niet ver! Ga hier rechtdoor,
neem de tweede straat links, en dan ga je over de brug.
Het is aan de rechterkant.
= Ah, it's not far! Go straight here,
take the second street left, and then go over the bridge.
It's on the right side.

YOU: Hoeveel minuten lopen is het?
= How many minutes walking is it?

STRANGER: Vijf minuten, misschien tien.
= Five minutes, maybe ten.

YOU: Dank u wel!
= Thank you!

STRANGER: Geen probleem! Veel plezier!
= No problem! Have fun!

steps.note:
Very common response to requests.
'Kun je me helpen?' → 'Ja, natuurlijk!'
Identical to German 'natürlich'.

'Veel' = much/many. 'Plezier' = pleasure/fun.
Said when someone is going somewhere nice.
From French 'plaisir'.

steps.q: 'Ik zoek het station.' — why no preposition after 'zoek'?
steps.hint: Unlike English 'look FOR' — Dutch just says 'ik zoek...'

steps.q: 'Weet u waar het station is?' — 'weten' means:

steps.s: Ga rechtdoor, neem de tweede straat {1}, en ga over de {2}.
steps.hint: Straight, second street LEFT, over the BRIDGE
Do you KNOW WHERE the station is?

steps.q: A Dutch person says 'Veel plezier!' — this means:

steps.title: Unit 6: Alles Samen
steps.desc: Bikes, trains, directions, prepositions, separable verbs, 'even', asking for help — all of Unit 6 in one final test. The scenario: a day trip through the Netherlands using every form of transport.
goals: Prepositions (op, in, aan, bij, naar, van, met)

steps.title: 📋 What You Know Now
steps.text:
After Unit 6 you can:

• Say how you travel (fiets, trein, auto, lopen)
• Use gaan (to go) in all forms
• Give and understand directions
• Use 8 prepositions correctly
• Board, exit, and transfer on trains
• Ask 'where is...?' and 'how do I get there?'
• Use 'even' like a local
• Ask strangers for help politely

You can now navigate the entire Netherlands.

steps.q: 'Ik ga met de fiets naar het werk.' — how many prepositions?

steps.hint: Step IN, step OUT, step OVER

steps.s: De trein {1} om drie uur. Wij stappen in Utrecht {2}.

steps.hint: Just quickly — the most Dutch word

steps.s: ___ is er een apotheek in de buurt?
steps.hint: Polite conversation starter before a question

steps.s: Ik koop brood {1} de bakker. De melk is {2} de koelkast.
steps.hint: AT the bakery. IN the fridge.

steps.q: There are more bikes than people in NL because:
